1: hallĕlû-yāh hallĕlû ʾet-yhwh min-haššāmayim hallĕlûhû bammĕrômîm — "Praise the LORD! Praise the LORD from the heavens; praise him in the heights above!" The opening triple use of hallĕlû (praise — Piel imperative plural of hālal; the root of Hallel and Hallelujah) immediately establishes the cosmic register. min-haššāmayim (from the heavens) — the praise is summoned from above; heaven is not merely where God dwells but from where praise is called down. bammĕrômîm (in the heights above — from rûm, to be high; the superlative elevation). The structure of the psalm (vv 1–6 heaven; vv 7–12 earth; vv 13–14 climax) creates a praise that moves from the highest point of creation downward to encompass everything.
2: hallĕlûhû kol-mallāʾkāyw hallĕlûhû kol-ṣĕbāʾōtāyw — "Praise him, all his angels! Praise him, all his hosts/heavenly armies!" mallāʾkāyw (his angels/messengers — from mālāʾk, messenger, envoy; the divine attendants who serve in the heavenly court). ṣĕbāʾōtāyw (his hosts/armies — ṣābāʾ, host or army; the term for the divine warrior's forces, as in the divine title YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt, "LORD of hosts"). The angelic host is called to praise before the natural order; the heavenly court leads the cosmic liturgy. Psalms 103:20–21 uses the same pairing: "bless the LORD, all his angels... all his hosts, his ministers."
3: hallĕlûhû šemeš wĕyārēaḥ hallĕlûhû kol-kôkĕbê ʾôr — "Praise him, sun and moon; praise him, all you shining stars!" The luminaries of Genesis 1:14–18 — šemeš (sun, the great light), yārēaḥ (moon, the lesser light) — are called to praise. kôkĕbê ʾôr (stars of light/shining stars) — all the lesser luminaries. The sun and moon were among the most prominent objects of ANE worship (Shamash the sun-god; Sin the moon-god in Mesopotamia; Shemesh in Canaan). Ps 148's call to these astral bodies to praise YHWH is an implicit polemic: the objects other nations worship are themselves worshippers, summoned to praise the one who made them.
4: hallĕlûhû šĕmê haššāmayim wĕhammayim ʾăšer mēʿal haššāmayim — "Praise him, you highest heavens, and you waters above the heavens!" šĕmê haššāmayim (heavens of the heavens, the superlative — literally "skies of the skies") — the divine abode beyond the created heavens (Deuteronomy 10:14; 1 Kings 8:27; Psalms 115:16). hammayim ʾăšer mēʿal haššāmayim (the waters above the heavens) — the cosmological upper waters of Genesis 1:7 that are above the rāqîaʿ (firmament). The psalm takes the full ANE cosmological model and calls every tier of it to praise. The waters that were separated at creation (Genesis 1:6–7) now join in the praise-order rather than threatening the ordered world.
5: yĕhallĕlû ʾet-šēm yhwh kî hûʾ ṣiwwāh wĕnibrāʾû — "let them praise the name of the LORD, for he commanded and they were created." ṣiwwāh (commanded — Piel perfect of ṣāwāh; the decisive divine command) + nibrāʾû (and they were created — Niphal perfect of bārāʾ, the distinctive creation verb used in Genesis 1:1). The creation formula: the word and the act are simultaneous. This is the deepest possible basis for praise: the praising creatures exist because of the divine command. The kî (for/because) clause makes this the warrant for all cosmic praise — every creature praises because it was commanded into existence.
6: wayyaʿămîdēm lāʿad lĕʿôlām ḥōq-nātan wĕlōʾ yaʿăbôr — "he has established them forever and ever; he has issued a decree that cannot be transgressed." wayyaʿămîdēm (he established/stationed them — Hiphil wayyiqtol of ʿāmad, to stand; the causative: he made them stand permanently). lāʿad lĕʿôlām (forever and ever — the doubled temporal expression for eternity). ḥōq (decree/statute — the fixed law governing the regular behavior of natural phenomena; the same word used in Jeremiah 5:22 where YHWH gives the sea its boundary-decree). wĕlōʾ yaʿăbôr (and it will not pass away/be transgressed — ʿābar, to pass over, exceed, transgress). The regularity of the seasons, the fixed paths of the stars — all governed by YHWH's unchangeable decree.
7: hallĕlû ʾet-yhwh min-hāʾāreṣ tannînîm wĕkol-tĕhōmôt — "Praise the LORD from the earth, you sea monsters and all ocean depths!" The shift to the earth-section (vv 7–12) begins with the most dramatic reversal: tannînîm (sea dragons/monsters — plural of tannîn; the term used for the chaos-creatures in Psalms 74:13–14 ["You divided the sea by your might; you broke the heads of the sea monsters"] and Job 7:12). In ANE mythology these were the primordial chaos beings YHWH conquered at creation (Leviathan, Tiamat). Here they are called to praise — the chaos-monsters are worshippers, not threats. tĕhōmôt (ocean deeps, from tĕhôm — the deep of Genesis 1:2, cognate with Akkadian Tiamat) — the depths called to praise alongside the monsters they harbor.
8: ʾēš ûbārād šeleg wĕqîṭôr rûaḥ sĕʿārāh ʿōśāh dĕbārô — "fire and hail, snow and mist, stormy wind fulfilling his word." Four elements of violent weather: ʾēš (fire — lightning), bārād (hail — the same term in the plagues of Exodus 9:18–26), šeleg (snow), qîṭôr (mist/vapor/smoke — used of the smoke of incense, Genesis 19:28). The unifying phrase is ʿōśāh dĕbārô (fulfilling/doing his word — participle of ʿāśāh): the violent weather phenomena are not chaos but servants executing YHWH's commands. The same logic as the divine warrior's weapons: natural catastrophe as divine instrument (cf. Psalms 147:15–18 where the divine word commands weather).
9: hehārîm wĕkol-gĕbāʿôt ʿêṣ pĕrî wĕkol-ʾărazîm — "mountains and all hills, fruit trees and all cedars." The geographical and botanical inventory: hārîm (mountains, the great elevations) + gĕbāʿôt (hills, the lesser heights) — the entire relief of the land. ʿêṣ pĕrî (fruit tree — literally "tree of fruit," the cultivated orchard) + ʾărazîm (cedars — the ʾerez, the great wild tree of Lebanon, symbol of height and majesty, Psalms 92:12; Psalms 104:16). The cultivated and wild botanical realms both praise.
10: haḥayyāh wĕkol-bĕhēmāh remeś wĕṣippôr kānāp — "wild beasts and all livestock, creeping things and flying birds." The standard zoological categories of the Hebrew world: ḥayyāh (wild beast, living thing — the untamed animals of the field) + bĕhēmāh (livestock/domestic animals) + remes (creeping thing — the crawling creatures of the earth, Genesis 1:24) + ṣippôr kānāp (bird of wing — a compound intensifying the flight characteristic; every winged flier). The four animal categories match the creation taxonomy of Genesis 1:20–25.
11: malkê-ʾereṣ wĕkol-lĕʾummîm śārîm wĕkol-šōpĕṭê āreṣ — "kings of the earth and all peoples, princes and all judges of the earth." The political order is called to praise. malkê-ʾereṣ (kings of the earth) — the highest human authority; lĕʾummîm (peoples, nations — plural of lĕʾom, a people-group; distinct from gôy in that lĕʾom emphasizes the communal solidarity). śārîm (princes, officials — the secondary power-holders) + šōpĕṭîm (judges — the judicial authority). Every tier of human governance is subsumed under the praise of YHWH; the same kings who issue decrees are themselves called to declare the greatness of the one who issues cosmic decrees (v6).
12: bĕḥûrîm wĕgam-bĕtûlôt zĕqēnîm ʿim-nĕʿārîm — "young men and also young women together, old men with children." The age spectrum of humanity: bĕḥûrîm (young men — the vigorous, marriageable males) + bĕtûlôt (young women/virgins — from bĕtûlāh, a young woman of marriageable age) + zĕqēnîm (old men/elders — the socially senior) + nĕʿārîm (children/young ones — the socially junior). The gam (also, even) before the women marks an inclusiveness: not just men but also women. This age-and-gender inclusiveness completes the human catalog before the climactic verses.
13: yĕhallĕlû ʾet-šēm yhwh kî-niśgāb šĕmô lĕbaddô hôdô ʿal-ʾereṣ wĕšāmāyim — "let them praise the name of the LORD, for his name alone is exalted; his splendor is above earth and heaven." niśgāb šĕmô (his name is exalted/set on high — Niphal of śāgab, to be inaccessibly high, beyond reach) — the name that is beyond the reach of any competing name. lĕbaddô (alone, by himself, exclusively — from bādad, to be separate/isolated; the emphasis on YHWH's exclusive majesty). hôdô (his splendor/majesty — from hôd, the majestic presence; related to the visible glory) ʿal-ʾereṣ wĕšāmāyim (above earth and heaven) — a merism: the entire created order is encompassed and transcended by YHWH's glory.
14: wayyārem qeren lĕʿammô tĕhillāh lĕkol-ḥăsîdāyw libĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʿam-qĕrōbô hallĕlû-yāh — "he has raised high the horn of his people, the praise of all his faithful ones, of the children of Israel, the people near to him. Praise the LORD!" wayyārem qeren (he raised/lifted the horn — Hiphil wayyiqtol of rûm, to raise; qeren, horn — the horn as the symbol of strength and honor; to have one's horn raised = to be vindicated, exalted, given honor; 1 Samuel 2:10; Psalms 89:17, 24; Psalms 132:17). The horn raised for ʿammô (his people) is the covenant-community's vindication, not just an individual's. ḥăsîdāyw (his devoted/faithful ones — from ḥesed; those who embody covenant loyalty, the same community as in Psalms 149:1, 5, 9). ʿam-qĕrōbô (the people near to him — from qārab, to draw near; the priestly term for approaching YHWH in the sanctuary, here describing Israel's covenantal intimacy with YHWH). The closing hallĕlû-yāh seals the cosmic praise with the covenant community's own doxological word.
